name: Stakeholder Mapping
summary: A framework for identifying and prioritising all the people and groups who have an interest in a political decision — and determining how to engage with each.
whenToUseIt: When developing policy, building support for legislation, managing a political issue, planning community engagement, or preparing for a contentious decision.
steps: List all stakeholders affected by the decision — individuals, organisations, communities, media, other politicians
Map each stakeholder on two axes: level of influence (high/low) and level of interest (high/low)
High influence + high interest = Key Players: engage closely, consult regularly, manage actively
High influence + low interest = Context Setters: keep informed, don't overwhelm, activate when needed
Low influence + high interest = Subjects: keep informed, provide channels to contribute, don't over-promise
Low influence + low interest = Crowd: monitor only, communicate broadly
Develop a tailored engagement strategy for each quadrant
example: A state government proposing a new public transport line would map: Key Players — the Transport Minister, Treasury, affected local councils, construction unions, major contractors. Context Setters — the media, the federal government (co-funding), the opposition transport spokesperson. Subjects — commuters, local businesses, environmental groups, disability advocates. Crowd — the general public in unaffected areas. Each group requires different communication: Key Players need face-to-face briefings; Subjects need public consultation sessions; the Crowd gets a media campaign.
